lesson_title: Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.text: {{c1::Type conversion}} is the process of converting a value from one type to another.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.front: What's the difference between implicit and explicit type conversion?
cards.back:
• Implicit: Compiler converts automatically
  • Explicit: Programmer requests conversion with a cast
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.question: What type conversion happens here?
cards.answer: Implicit conversion from int to double
cards.explanation: The integer literal 5 is implicitly converted to 5.0 (double) by the compiler.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.text: To explicitly convert a value, use {{c1::static_cast<new_type>(expression)}}.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.question: What does x equal?
cards.explanation: static_cast<int> converts 5.7 to int by truncating the decimal part.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.concept: Why use static_cast instead of C-style casts
cards.explanation:
Prefer static_cast because:
  • More visible in code (easier to find)
  • Compile-time checked for validity
  • More restrictive (safer)
  • Clearer intent
Avoid C-style casts like (int)x.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.text: A {{c1::narrowing conversion}} is a conversion where data may be lost, such as converting double to int.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.question: Why might this cause a warning?
cards.answer: Narrowing conversion from double to int
cards.explanation: Brace initialization disallows implicit narrowing conversions. Use static_cast to make it explicit.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.back:
• Converting between numeric types
  • Making implicit conversions explicit
  • Converting to/from char
  • Whenever you need an explicit conversion
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.question: How can you fix this to get 3.5?
cards.explanation: Cast at least one operand to double before division to get floating point result.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.text: Converting from larger signed to smaller unsigned type (e.g., int to unsigned char) can cause {{c1::overflow}} and should use {{c2::static_cast}} to be explicit.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast

cards.front: What conversions can static_cast NOT perform?
cards.back:
• Cannot cast away const
  • Cannot perform pointer to unrelated type conversions
  • Cannot do dynamic type checking
Use const_cast, reinterpret_cast, or dynamic_cast for these.
cards.source: 4.12 - Introduction to type conversion and static_cast
